turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Hi, doctor. I’m here for my blood pressure check, but I’ve also had these on-and-off headaches for about three weeks.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: Hello, thanks for coming in. We’ll look at both. Tell me a bit about the headaches first. Where do you feel them, and what do they feel like?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Mostly at the front or around my temples. It’s more like a dull pressure. It comes and goes.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: Any nausea, vomiting, blurry vision, dizziness, weakness, or trouble speaking with the headaches?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: No, none of that. Just the headache. Sometimes I feel a bit tense with work.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: Okay. How bad is it out of ten, and how long does each one last?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Maybe a four or five. It can last a few hours. I usually just take some water or rest.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: Have you noticed anything that brings it on, like stress, poor sleep, caffeine, or missing meals?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Stress, I think. Work has been busy at school. And I do skip lunch sometimes.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: That makes sense. How have your blood pressure readings been lately, if you’ve checked them at home? And are you taking the amlodipine every day?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: I’ve been taking it daily. I don’t check at home very often, though. Last time at the pharmacy it was a bit high, I think.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: Any chest pain, shortness of breath, swelling in the legs, or headaches that wake you from sleep?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: No chest pain or shortness of breath. No swelling either. The headaches don’t wake me up.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: Good. Have you been taking anything else for the headaches, like pain tablets, nasal sprays, or extra caffeine?

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Just the cetirizine when my allergies act up. I haven’t really taken pain tablets for it.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: Thanks. I’d like to check your blood pressure today and have a quick look at you. Based on what you’ve said, this sounds more like tension or stress headaches, but we’ll confirm your pressure first.

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: Okay. I was worried it might mean the blood pressure is getting worse.

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: It’s understandable to worry. We’ll check it properly today. After that, if the reading is up, we may adjust your treatment or ask for home readings over the next week. For the headaches, regular meals, good hydration, sleep, and less stress can help. If needed, you can use paracetamol as directed, unless you’ve been told not to.

turns.speaker: patient
turns.text: That sounds fair. Should I be doing anything else at home?

turns.speaker: doctor
turns.text: Yes. Try to keep a simple blood pressure log if you can, take your amlodipine daily, and come back sooner if the headaches become severe, sudden, or different, or if you get vision changes, weakness, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Today’s impression is that your headaches are likely tension-related, with routine blood pressure review needed. We’ll check your blood pressure now, then decide on follow-up depending on the result.
